contents: ORIGINAL ARTICLERisk for hepatitis B and C virusreactivation in patients with psoriasis onbiologic therapies: A retrospectivecohort study and systematic review ofthe literatureIgor Snast, MD,a Lihi Atzmony, MD,a Marius Braun, MD,b,c Emmilia Hodak, MD,a,c andLev Pavlovsky, MD, PhDaPetach Tikva and Tel Aviv, IsraelBackground: Patients with psoriasis on biologic therapies and a history of viral hepatitis carry a risk forreactivation.Objective: We evaluated safety of biologic therapies in psoriasis patients seropositive for hepatitis B or Cviruses (HBV, HCV).Methods: A retrospective cohort study design was used. Clinical and laboratory data for 30 patientsundergoing biologic therapy who were seropositive for HBV or HCV were evaluated. Next, a systematicreview was performed. Primary outcomes were hepatitis and viral reactivation during therapy. Treatmentduration and antiviral prophylaxis were also recorded.Results: Serology indicated HCV infection in 4 patients, past HBV infection in 17 patients, isolated coreantibody in 8 patients, and chronic HBV infection in 1 patient. During follow-up (mean 4.85 6 3.1 years),no patients experienced hepatitis or viral reactivation. The systematic review of the literature included 49studies comprising 312 patients followed for a mean of 30.9 months. Viral reactivation occurred in 2/175patients who were seropositive for core antibody and 3/97 with HCV infection (yearly rates, 0.32% and2.42%, respectively) compared with 8/40 patients with chronic HBV infection (yearly rate, 13.92%). Threeof these 8 patients with reactivated HBV infection received antiviral prophylaxis.

Limitations: We pooled heterogeneous studies evaluating different biologic therapies.Conclusion: Biologic therapies pose minimal risk for viral reactivation in low-risk patients withouthepatitis seropositive for HCV or HBV core antibody but are a considerable risk in patients with chronicHBV infection, highlighting the necessity of antiviral prophylaxis. ( J Am Acad Dermatol Key words: biologic; HBcAg; hepatitis B virus; hepatitis C virus; psoriasis; reactivation; risk.In the last 15 years, biologic therapies have beenintroduced for the treatment of moderate-to-severe psoriasis.1 The main therapies avail-able in Israel are antietumor necrosis factor-a(TNF-a), the interleukin (IL) 12/23 p40 monoclonalantibody ustekinumab, and the recently approvedIL-17 antibody secukinumab.2 Because these agentslack direct hepatic or renal toxicity,3 these selectiveimmunomodulatory treatments are appealing topatients with concurrent viral hepatitis.From the Department of Dermatology, Rabin Medical CenterBeilinson Hospital, Petach Tikvaa; Liver Institute, Rabin MedicalCenter Beilinson Hospital, Petach Tikvab; and Sackler Faculty ofMedicine, Tel Aviv University, Tel Aviv.cFunding sources: None.Conflicts of interest: None declared.Accepted for publication January 24, 2017.Reprintrequests: LevPavlovsky,MD,PhD,DepartmentofDermatology, Rabin Medical Center Beilinson Hospital, 39Jabotinsky St, Petach Tikva 4941492, Israel. E-mail: online May 8, 2017.0190-9622/$36.00 2017 by the American Academy of Dermatology, Inc.

With the advent of TNF-aeinhibitor use in pa-tients, so did reports on various disseminated andopportunistic infections.4,5 TNF-a is an importantcytokineinhostantiviraldefense.6Reportsdescribing hepatitis B virus (HBV) reactivationfollowing TNF-aeinhibitor use in patients withrheumatoidarthritisandspondyloarthropathies7promptedconcernsregarding the safety of allbiologics in psoriasis patientsexposed to HBV or hepatitisC virus (HCV), but no sys-tematic study addressing thisissue has been conducted todate.The aim of the presentstudy was to evaluate thesafetyprofileofbiologictherapiesamongpsoriasispatients with concurrent pos-itive serology for HBV orHCV.

METHODSRetrospective cohortstudyA retrospective cohort study design was used. Theelectronic database of the Dermatology Departmentof Rabin Medical Center, a tertiary referral hospital inIsrael, was searched for patients treated with biologicagents for moderate-to-severe psoriasis since 2005.All patients were screened for HBV and HCV beforeinitiation of biologic therapy. Positive HBV serologywas subdivided into 3 categories according to theEuropean Association for the Study of Liver Disease8:1) resolved past HBV infection (HBV surface antigen[HBsAg] negative, HBV surface-specific antibody[HBsAb], and HBV core-specific antibody [HBcAb]positive), 2) chronic HBV infection (HBsAg positive),and 3) only core antibody positive (HBsAg negative,HBsAb negative, and HBcAb positive). Hepatitis wasdefined as an increase in the alanine transaminaselevel to 5 times the upper limit of normal.9 Becauseno uniform diagnostic criteria are available for HBVreactivation, we adopted the definition commonlyused by other researchers10: an increase in HBVreplication of at least 1 log 10 copies/mL or conver-sion of serum HBV DNA results from negative topositive. HCVreactivation was defined as an increaseof at least 1 log 10 copies/mL. According to Israeliguidelines, patients in whom pretreatment serologywas suggestive of HBV or HCV infection werescheduled for clinical evaluation, which includedmeasurement of transaminases, every 2-3 monthsuntil the end of follow-up. Patients with chronic HBV

infection were serologically evaluated every 3-6 months. Because the best strategy for monitoringlow-risk, only core antibodyepositive patients isunknown,11 the serologic status (HBsAg, HBcAb,and HBV DNA) of patients was evaluated at variousintervals. The presence of HCV RNA was assessed atbaseline and reassessed as part of antiviral therapeu-tic protocols, as suggested bythe hepatologist. The demo-graphic, clinical, and labora-tory data were extracted ontoa chart, which included thefields shown in Tables I-IV.The study was approved bythe local ethics committee.

Systematic literaturereviewThesystematicreviewwas conducted and reportedinaccordancewiththePRISMA (Preferred Report-ingItemsforSystemicReviews and Meta-Analyses)statement.12 We performed asearch with no date limitsusing PubMed, our institute's search engine, andreference lists of included articles on May 13, 2016.The search criteria are shown in Appendix I (avail-able at ).Inclusion and exclusion criteria. We includedarticles written in English and excluded articleswritten in other languages. We selected articlesdescribing the treatment of psoriasis with at least 1biologic agent in the presence of a positive serologicor virologic marker for HBVor HCV before treatmentonset that also had available serologic or virologicdata on HBV or HCV at the end of follow-up. Anyrelevant study, regardless of design, was included.Literature reviews, expert opinions, and studies inwhich data of interest could not be extracted wereexcluded.Data extraction and variables. One reviewer(IS) screened the titles and abstracts of all retrievedarticles followed by the full text of the articlesconsidered potentially eligible and extracted thedata into a predefined form (Supplemental Table I;available at )13-60 that includedthe following items: 1) HCVand HBV infection statusat the beginning of the study, 2) treatment factors,such as treatment duration, types, and quantities ofdifferent biologics, and 3) evidence of viral reactiva-tion or hepatitis.Studies were graded according to their level ofevidence: (A) prospective controlled study, (B)CAPSULE SUMMARYd Patients with a history of viral hepatitiscarry a reactivation risk, but it isuncertain how this is affected by biologictherapies for psoriasis.d Patients positive for hepatitis C virus orhepatitis B virus (HBV) core antibodywithout evidence of hepatitis had aminimal risk for reactivation, whereasthose with chronic HBV had a higher riskfor reactivation.

d Select patients with a history of viralhepatitis may be safely treated withbiologics.retrospective study, or (C) case report or caseseries.61Data analysisPatients were divided into 3 groups: those withHCV infection; those with chronic HBV infection,defined as serologically positive for surface antigen;and those positive for the HBV core antibody,irrespective of surface antibody status. To calculatethe viral reactivation, we included only patients withavailable HBV DNA levels at the end of follow-up.Data were analyzed with descriptive statistics.Categorical variables are described with numbersand percentages, and quantitative variables aredescribed with means and standard deviations orranges.RESULTSRetrospective cohort studyPatients. Of the 207 patients with psoriasistreated with biologics during the study period, 30(14.49%) had positive markers of HBV (n = 26) orHCV (n = 4) before treatment initiation.Hepatitis B virus infection. The 26 patientswith serologic evidence of HBV infection included18 men and 8 women of mean age 56.3 (range 34-80)years. Their clinical (Table I) and laboratory (TableII) data are shown. All had long-standing plaquepsoriasis (average duration 14.6 years, range 0.5-40 years); 2 patients (cases 7 and 14) also had a phaseoferythrodermaduringdiseaseexacerbation.Twenty patients previously had used $1 nonbio-logic agent including methotrexate or cyclosporine.Etanercept was the most common biologic agentused (n = 24), followed by adalimumab (n = 15),ustekinumab (n = 11), infliximab (n = 4), secukinu-mab (n = 3), golimumab (n = 1), and alefacept (n = 1each). Fifteen patients received [1 biologic agent(average 2.27, range 1-6). The mean duration oftreatment including clinical follow-up was 5.31(range 1-11) years.Seventeen patients had serologic findings sugges-tive of a resolved past infection (HBsAg negative,

HBsAb positive, HBcAb positive); 8 patients wereseropositive for only core antibody (HBsAg negative,HBsAb negative, and HBcAb positive), and 1 patientwas an inactive HBV carrier (HBsAg positive, HBV eantigen negative, and serum HBV DNA 122 IU/mL).No other patient had detectable serum HBV DNA atbaseline or concurrent HCV infection. Two patients(cases 14 and 23) received antiviral prophylaxis withlamivudine 100 mg daily during treatment.During follow-up, significant changes in serumliver enzyme activity, clinical signs of hepatitis, andliver failure were not found. Data on HBV viral loadand serology at the end of follow-up were availablefor 25 patients (mean follow-up time 4.45 years,range 1-10.3 years). No patients had evidence of viralreactivation or HBsAg seroconversion.A Psoriasis Area Severity Index 50 improvementfrom baseline was achieved in 18 patients (69%) at3 months from the start of treatment. Several adverseeffects were recorded during follow-up: case 3 hadpneumonia, case 5 had 2 episodes of upper respira-tory infection, and case 23 had a myocardialinfarction.Hepatitis C virus infection. The patients withserologic evidence of HCV infection were 4 men ofmean age 62.3 (range 38-78) years. Their clinical(Table III) and laboratory (Table IV) data are shown.All had longstanding plaque psoriasis (averageduration 32.3 years, range 15-60 years). One patient(case 1) had used methotrexate and cyclosporinebefore biologic therapy. All received treatment withetanercept, and one (case 1) was also treatedsequentially with adalimumab. Mean duration oftreatment and clinical follow-up was 1.86 (range0.67-3) years. Data on the viral load at the end offollow-up were available for 3 patients (meanfollow-up time 1.47 years, range 0.67-2.17 years).The viral isotype was available for 2 patients: 1 hadgenotype 2a2c and the other had genotype 1b. Nopatients received antiviral treatment. Serology in onepatient (case 4) was also suggestive of past HBVinfection, with borderline positivity for core anti-body and positivity for HBsAb.No patients exhibited any substantial change inhepatic enzymes or evidence of viral reactivation.Three patients achieved Psoriasis Area SeverityIndex 50 at 3 months, and no patients had severeadverse effects caused by treatment.

Systematic literature review and analysisIncluded studies. Our search yielded 362 cita-tions. Forty-eight already published studies and ourdata fulfilled the inclusion criteria (Fig 1) for a total of49 studies. From our cohort study, we included onlythe 28 patients with available HBV DNA levels at theAbbreviations used:HBcAb:HBV core-specific antibodyHBsAb:HBV surface-specific antibodyHBsAg:HBV surface antigenHBV:hepatitis B virusHCV:hepatitis C virusIL:interleukinOBI:occult HBV infectionPRISMA:Preferred Reporting Items for SystemicReviews and Meta-AnalysesTNF-a:tumor necrosis factor-aend of follow-up. One study was prospective (gradeA, 2 patients); 15 were retrospective (grade B, 265patients), and 33 were either small case series or casereports (grade C, 45 patients). Sample sizes rangedfrom 1 to 62, with a total of 803.48 reported patient-years: HCV infected patients accounted for 123.83patient-years, chronic HBV infected patients ac-counted for 57.46 patient-years, and patients positivefor HBcAb accounted for 622.19 patient-years. Thecompletelistofincludedstudiesappearsin

Appendix I (Supplemental Table I; available ).13-60 Table V13-60 summarizesthe main characteristics of the included studies, andTable VI summarizes the reports describing viralreactivation during biologic therapy.ChronicHBVinfection. In40patients,serology was compatible with chronic HBV infec-tion. Mean follow-up time was 17.24 (range 0.75-39)months. On average, patients received 1.05 (range 1-2) different biologics. Viral reactivation was docu-mented in 8 (20%) patients, and concurrent hepatitisin 1 patient, for a calculated yearly reactivation rateof 13.92%. Of the 26 patients with chronic HBV whoreceived antiviral prophylaxis, 3 had viral reactiva-tion, for an adjusted yearly reactivation rate of 7.74%,compared with 5/14 patients who did not receiveprophylaxis, for an adjusted yearly reactivation rateof 26.31% (Supplemental Table II; available at ).Resolved past HBV infection or core anti-body only. Serology was positive for core antibodyin 175 patients. Mean follow-up time was 42.66(range 7.3-66) months. On average, patients received1.4 (range 1-2.32) different biologics. Two patientsreceived antiviral prophylaxis. Two patients (1.14%)had evidence of viral reactivation, for a yearlyreactivation rate of 0.32%. None of the patients hadhepatitis.HCV infection. Serology was suggestive of HCVinfection in 97 patients (Table V).13-60 Mean follow-up time was 15.32 (range 3-46) months. None of the 3 patients had evidence of hepatitisor received antiviral prophylaxis.

DISCUSSIONUp to 37.2% of patients with moderate-to-severepsoriasis are treated with biologictherapies.63Therefore, it is crucial to define the safety profile ofthese agents in patients with concurrent serologysuggestive of viral hepatitis.Our cohort included low-risk patients withoutevidence of hepatitis who were seropositive for HBVor HCV. None of these patients experienced hepatitisor viral reactivation, supporting the safety of usingbiologics in this setting. HBcAbViral load (IU/mL)AST (IU/L)ALT (IU/L)AntiviralPRXBaselineFollow-upBaselineFollow-up(months)BaselineEndBaselineEndThere were no serious adverse effects of biologic therapies.ALT, Alanine aminotransferase; AST, aspartate aminotransferase; HBcAb, HBV core antibody; HBsAb, HBV surface antibody; HBV, hepatitis Bvirus; N, within normal range; N/A, not available; PRX, prophylaxis.*Patient was seronegative for HBV e antigen.by the strengths of our study: longer mean follow-upperiod (5.31 for HBV; 1.86 for HCV; 4.85 yearsoverall) than in any previous cohort study; largeraverage number of biologic agents (2.13) thancalculated in our systematic review (1.24); and firstsafety assessment of the recently approved IL-17antibody, secukinumab, used by 3 patients withpositive markers for HBV.All patients with a history of exposure to HBVmight be at risk for viral reactivation during immu-nosuppressive states.64-69 Core antibody only posi-tivity is a clinically distinct entity from occultCaseAnti-HCVViral load (copies/mL)Viral isotypeAST (IU/L)ALT (IU/L)Anti viral PRXBaselineFollow-up (months)BaselineEndBaselineEndALT, Alanine aminotransferase; AST, aspartate aminotransferase; HCV, hepatitis C virus; N/A, not available; PRX, prophylaxis.

infection, which is defined by the persistence of HBVDNA in the liver tissue or serum without apparentinfection.70 Positivity for only core antibody mightindicate a resolved past infection, an occult HBVinfection (OBI), a false-positive HBcAb result, or areplicativeHBsAg-negativeinfection.71-73OBIscannot be confirmed because diagnosis requiresliver biopsies being positive for HBV DNA, and theperformance of a liver biopsy is not justifiable inpatients without clinical signs of hepatitis. Patientsregarded as having a past but resolved HBV infection(HBsAg negative, HBcAb positive, HBsAb positive)might instead have an infection with a low viralreplication rate.67 Because viral reactivation in thisgroup occurred primarily in patients with hemato-logic and lymphoproliferative malignancies treatedwith rituximab,66,67 these patients may be regardedas having potential OBIs. Therefore, in our system-atic review, patients seropositive for core antibodywere grouped together, irrespective of their surfaceantibody status.In our systematic review, only 2/175 patientspositive for core antibody (1.14%) had viral reac-tivation, for a 0.32% yearly rate. A recent systematicreviewincluding712rheumaticpatientswithresolved HBV infection treated with biologic thera-pies reported a reactivation rate of 1.7%.62 The lowercalculated risk in our review might reflect the lessfrequent use of concomitant immunosuppressants inpsoriasis than in rheumatic diseases.13The European Association for the Study of theLiver8 defines chronic HBV as surface antigen posi-tivity, regardless of the status of other serologicmarkers. Patients with chronic HBV infection are,by far, at greatest risk for HBV reactivation,74 withreported rates of up to 50% in those undergoingcytotoxic chemotherapy for malignancies withoutantiviralprophylaxis.75OursystematicreviewStudyViral profileNo ptsLevel ofevidenceBiologic therapiesLength oftreatment,monthsViralPRXHepatitis

ADA, Adalimumab; ALE, alefacept; EFA, efalizumab; ETA, etanercept; GOL, golimumab; HBcAb, HBV core antibody; HBV, hepatitis B virus; HCV,hepatitis C virus; INF, infliximab; SEC, secukinumab; UST, ustekinumab; PRX, prophylaxis; pts, patients; SD, standard deviation.included 40 chronic HBV patients, of whom 8 (20%)experienced viral reactivation, for a yearly reactiva-tion rate of 13.92%.In immunosuppressed patients with chronic HBVinfection, oral anti-HBV agents have proven to beeffective in reducing acute exacerbations and pre-venting fatal complications.8,76 Accordingly, only 3/26 patients in the systematic review with chronicHBV receiving antiviral prophylaxis experiencedviral reactivation compared with 5/14 patients withchronic HBV without prophylaxis, yielding yearlyreactivation rates of 7.74% and 26.31%, respectively(Table VI and Supplemental Table II; available ). Therefore, we suggest that allpatients with chronic HBV be treated with antiviralagents before biologics are initiated. This is sup-ported by the 2015 European guidelines for the useof anti-TNF therapy for psoriasis, wherein HBVinfection is not considered a barrier to immunosup-pression therapy.77Chronic HCV infection might be promoted by theupregulation of certain inflammatory cytokines, suchasTNF-a.78StudieshaveshownthatTNF-aeinhibitor therapy is associated with a drop in viralload and, in some cases, even lead to viral clear-ance.14,79 In our analysis, only 3/97 patients withHCV infection (3.09%) had evidence of viral reac-tivation, with a yearly rate of 2.42%. Considering that3%-10% of patients with HCV infection show spon-taneous fluctuations in viral load of [1 log HCVRNA,80 our systematic review suggests that biologictherapiesposenoriskofHCVreactivation.Nevertheless, because hepatocellular carcinoma81and viral reactivation are part of the natural historyof HCV, patients should be followed periodicallyaccording to the European Association for the Studyof the Liver guidelines.82

Our analysis has several limitations. First, differentbiologics,includingvariousagentswithintheantieTNF-a family, were shown to confer disparaterisk for serious infections among psoriasis patients.83Nonetheless, we preemptively decided to pool datafrom heterogeneous studies evaluating different bi-ologics to make possible an analysis of viral reac-tivation (which is an uncommon event) among arelatively small number of patients. Moreover, as theaverage follow-up period was limited, large-scaleprospective trials evaluating distinct biologic thera-pies are in need to validate our conclusions. Second,we included case reports, which are known to have apositive-outcome publication bias.84,85 The casereports and small case series accounted for only14.42% (45/312) of the total patients but 41.7% (5/12)of the patients with viral reactivation. Nonetheless,the possible overestimation of reactivation ratesstrengthens our conclusions regarding the safetyprofile of biologic therapies.In conclusion, our study indicates that low-riskpatients without hepatitis who are seropositive forHBV core antibody only or HCV infection haveminimal risk or no additive risk, respectively, forviral reactivation. Consequently, antiviral prophy-laxis seems unnecessary.In contrast, chronic carriers of HBV have about a14% risk for yearly viral reactivation. These patientsneed to be carefully evaluated for the risk:benefitratio of biologic therapy and receive antiviralprophylaxis.
